label: ai
topic: A man cloned from 10000-year-old DNA on a space station discovers his origins
text:
Dave found out while holding a paper cup of something that tasted like it had been filtered through a sock.

"I'm not supposed to be the one telling you," the phlebotomist said, which is exactly how you want any life-altering revelation to begin.

We were in a room that hummed faintly, like it had a secret it couldn't quite keep. Tubes ran along the walls, carrying fluids or data or both. The phlebotomist—his name tag said L. RIVAS—kept glancing at the door as if expecting someone more qualified to materialize and take over.

"Telling me what?" Dave asked.

Rivas adjusted his gloves, then didn't touch anything. "There was a breach. In the archive."

"What archive?"

"The genetic vault," Rivas said, lowering his voice, which didn't help because we were the only ones there. "They keep… samples. Ancient ones. For study. For continuity."

Dave took a sip of the drink and immediately regretted it. "Okay."

"And you," Rivas said, pointing at Dave with a hesitation that made the gesture feel almost apologetic, "are one of those samples."

There was a pause in which the hum of the room seemed to lean in.

"I'm pretty sure I'm not," Dave said.

Rivas nodded. "Yes. That's the standard reaction."

Somewhere else, ten thousand years earlier, a man who would eventually be called Dave stood at the edge of a crevasse and tried not to look down.

The ground there was split like a cracked bowl, the earth opening into a darkness that swallowed sound. The air smelled of stone and something older, something that didn't have a name yet because language hadn't decided it needed one.

The shaman stood beside him, wrapped in skins that still carried the memory of the animals they had been. "You are chosen," the shaman said, which in that time meant both everything and nothing.

"I don't want to be," the man said, though the word want was still new, still soft at the edges.

The shaman smiled, or something like it. "The story wants you."

Encyclopedia Entry: Wheel, Invention of. The wheel is commonly cited as one of the most significant technological advancements in human history. Its origins are debated, but the earliest evidence dates to approximately 3500 BCE. The wheel enabled transportation, pottery, and mechanical innovation. It represents a shift from direct interaction with the environment to mediated movement through it.

Dave sat down. The chair made a small, obedient sound.

"So I'm a clone," he said.

Rivas winced. "That word has… connotations."

"What word would you prefer?"

"Replicated individual," Rivas said, as if offering a menu option.

"Clone is shorter," Dave said.

Rivas nodded, relieved to have something settled. "Yes."

"And the original me is—what? Dead?"

Rivas hesitated. "Statistically, yes."

Somewhere else, the original Dave—though he was not called that—took a step forward. The ground shifted under his weight, small stones slipping into the crevasse with a sound that seemed to take too long to arrive.

"Why me?" he asked.

The shaman crouched, pressing a hand to the earth. "Because you will be remembered."

"I don't want to be remembered," the man said.

"That is not how stories work," the shaman replied.

Encyclopedia Entry: Story, Nature of. A story is a structured account of events, real or imagined, used to convey meaning, preserve memory, and create shared understanding. Stories require participants: a teller, a listener, and a subject. Over time, the subject may become indistinguishable from the story itself.

Dave rubbed his face. "So someone broke into a vault and decided to make me."

Rivas nodded. "In simplified terms, yes."

"For what purpose?"

"That part is unclear," Rivas said. "There are investigations."

"Great," Dave said. "Love an investigation."

He stood, then sat back down, as if the act of standing required a version of himself that hadn't been briefed yet.

"Do I have—" he paused, searching for a word that wouldn't make this feel like a joke, "—history?"

Rivas brightened slightly. "Yes. Extensive. Genetic memory, cultural inference, reconstructed lineage."

"Do I remember any of it?"

"No."

"Then what's the point?"

Rivas opened his mouth, then closed it.

Somewhere else, the man stepped again, and this time the ground gave way. For a moment, he was suspended between surfaces, between what he had been and what he was about to become.

The shaman watched, expression unreadable.

As the man fell, he thought—not in words, not yet, but in something like them—that this was a mistake.

Encyclopedia Entry: Wheel, Revisited. The wheel allows for movement without walking, for distance without effort. It creates the illusion that one can go forward without leaving anything behind. This is not strictly true.

Dave looked at his hands, turning them over as if expecting to find seams.

"So I'm a story," he said.

Rivas considered this. "In a sense."

"A bad one," Dave said.

"An ongoing one," Rivas corrected.

Somewhere in the archive, data shifted. Somewhere in the past, a body struck the darkness and was absorbed into it. Somewhere in the present, Dave took another sip of the terrible drink and didn't notice the taste.

Stories, after all, have a way of continuing whether or not the participants agree.
